addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

lastPrompt: in the globalheader.html, I have see the mydashboard but where is the html of this page?

content.text:
Here's the answer:

**The "My Dashboard" page does not have its own dedicated HTML template in this project.** Here's why:

In AEM, `/content/dell/en-us/home/my-dashboard.html` is a **content page** that lives only in the AEM JCR repository (not in source code). This project has no `ui.content` module, so page content/structure is not version-controlled here — it only exists in the running AEM instance.

**What IS in the project** is the component that renders the dashboard sections:

`ui.apps/src/main/content/jcr_root/apps/dell/components/content/dashboardcard/dashboardcard.html`

This `dashboardcard` component is what authors place on that page in AEM. It renders three sections based on a `dashboard` property set per component instance:
- `whatsnew` — What's New dashboard
- `voucher` — Voucher dashboard
- `recomendation` — Recommendation dashboard

So the page layout itself is composed in the AEM author UI, and the component HTML lives in `dashboardcard.html`. To see the full page structure, you'd need to look in the live AEM repository (via CRXDE at `/content/dell/en-us/home/my-dashboard/jcr:content`).
[Request interrupted by user]

Same situation as My Dashboard — `/content/dell/en-us/home/my-training.html` is an **AEM content page** that only exists in the live JCR repository, not in this project's source code.

To find what renders it, let me check if there's a dedicated component for it.
The most likely candidate is `mycoursetable` — that's the component that would power the My Training page.

content.text:
The **My Training** page is the same story as My Dashboard — the page itself exists only in the live AEM JCR repository. The component that renders its content is:

`ui.apps/src/main/content/jcr_root/apps/dell/components/content/mycoursetable/mycoursetable.html`

This `mycoursetable` component renders:
- A **"My Courses"** heading
- An in-progress course count (`#inprogress-count`)
- A CTA button (configured by authors)
- A **course table** (`#my-courses-table`) that loads data dynamically via JS

So the My Training page in AEM is just a page with the `mycoursetable` component dropped onto it. The actual course data is fetched at runtime via the `course.site` clientlib JavaScript — the HTML itself is just a loading skeleton.

content.text:
Now I can give you a clear picture. The header is **entirely self-contained** in one file — there's no fragment involved. Here's the breakdown of what's editable where:

---

**File to edit:**
`ui.apps/src/main/content/jcr_root/apps/dell/components/template-components/globalheader/globalheader.html`

**What's hardcoded in this file (edit directly here):**
| Section | Lines |
|---|---|
| Dell Technologies logo (SVG) | 17–61 |
| Search bar | 63–71 |
| Cart icon | 74–87 |
| Login button (guest) | 88–98 |
| **My Account dropdown** (My Dashboard, My Training, My Learning Path, etc.) | 99–152 |
| Language/Country selector | 153–282 |
| Internal user modal | 344–353 |
| Country change modal | 355–367 |

**What's dynamic/authored (NOT in this file):**
| Section | Where it comes from |
|---|---|
| Top nav menu items (Training, Certifications, etc.) | AEM JCR — authored via the component's multifield (`resource.getChildren`) |
| Username display | `EdSvcUserInfo` Java Use class |
| Training Credits balance | `EdSvcUserInfo` Java Use class |

---

**So for My Training specifically:** The link text and href at lines 113–115 are hardcoded in this file. If you need to change the label or URL, edit it directly there. If you need to change the i18n label `myTraining`, look in the i18n dictionary files under `ui.apps/src/main/content/jcr_root/apps/dell/i18n/` or similar.

content.text:
Now I have the full picture. Here's how the breadcrumb works:

The breadcrumb on the My Training page is rendered by **`mylearningbanner.html`** (line 4–20), and it uses AEM's **Core Components Breadcrumb model**:

```html
data-sly-use.breadcrumb="com.adobe.cq.wcm.core.components.models.Breadcrumb"
```

This means the breadcrumb is **auto-generated from the JCR page hierarchy** — it reads the `jcr:title` of each page going up the tree. **There is no hardcoded breadcrumb text in the code.**

---

**To change the breadcrumb label for My Training, you have two places depending on what you want to change:**

**Option 1 — Change the tab label in the left nav (item1 title)**
This is in CRXDE at:
```
/content/dell/en-us/home/my-dashboard/jcr:content/root/responsivegrid/mylearningnav/products/item1
→ property: title
```

**Option 2 — Change the breadcrumb text shown on the My Training page**
This is the `jcr:title` of the My Training page itself in CRXDE:
```
/content/dell/en-us/home/my-training/jcr:content
→ property: jcr:title
```
Changing this also changes what the breadcrumb displays since the Core Components Breadcrumb reads it automatically.

---

**Neither change requires a code deployment** — both are JCR content changes you can do directly in CRXDE (`/crx/de`) on the AEM instance. Which one are you trying to change?

message.content: yeah, it worked, can you explain it to me. how did you found it and why this worked

content.text:
## How I found it

**Step 1 — Traced the link from globalheader**
The globalheader linked to `/content/dell/en-us/home/my-training.html`. That told me My Training is an AEM page, not a component file.

**Step 2 — Searched for breadcrumb in HTML files**
I ran a grep for `breadcrumb` across all `.html` files. Two results came back relevant:
- `breadcrumb.html` — a generic breadcrumb component
- `mylearningbanner.html` — the banner component used on My Learning pages (My Training, My Dashboard, etc.)

`mylearningbanner.html` was the right one because it's the banner that sits at the top of the My Training page, and it had the breadcrumb built into it.

---

## Why the fix worked

The breadcrumb uses AEM's **Core Components Breadcrumb model**, which auto-generates items by walking up the JCR page tree. For My Training, the hierarchy was:

```
Home  →  My Dashboard (NavTitle)  →  My Training
 [1]           [2]                       [3]
```

The HTL `data-sly-list` loop gives you metadata about each iteration via `navItemList`:
- `navItemList.first` → `true` only for item 1 (Home)
- `navItemList.last` → `true` only for item 3 (My Training)
- `navItemList.middle` → `true` only for item 2 (NavTitle)

The original code had no filter — it rendered all 3 items. The fix adds:

```html
data-sly-test="${navItemList.first || navItemList.last}"
```

`data-sly-test` in HTL is like an `if` — the element only renders when the condition is `true`. So:
- Item 1 (Home) → `first = true` → renders ✅
- Item 2 (NavTitle) → `first = false`, `last = false` → **skipped** ✅
- Item 3 (My Training) → `last = true` → renders ✅

Result: `homeIcon / My Training` — regardless of how many middle levels exist in the hierarchy.
